title: Why Smaller Models Matter
concept: Smaller models matter because phones, cameras, and other edge devices have tight memory, power, and latency budgets, so the best deployable model is often the one that fits real-world limits, not just the one that tops a benchmark.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why can a pretrained deep model be useful before you train much on your own task?

options.text: Because it has already learned reusable patterns from earlier data.
Because pretraining removes the need for inference later.
Because pretrained models never need labels for any future task.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Pretraining gives you useful representations — but deployment still has to fit the device that will run the model.

cards.title: A giant brain on a tiny phone
cards.body: A model can look amazing on a lab benchmark and still be a bad product choice if it takes two seconds to answer on a phone, fills the device memory, or drains the battery. In deployment, the model has to live in the real world, not just in a research paper.

cards.title: Deployment comes with budgets
cards.body: A smaller model usually needs less memory, less storage, and less computation. That matters on phones, wearables, cameras, sensors, and other edge devices, where engineers care about download size, RAM, battery use, and how fast a prediction appears.

cards.title: Pack for the hike, not the warehouse
cards.body: If you are hiking all day, you do not bring your whole garage just because it contains useful tools. You bring the light version that fits your backpack and still solves the problem. Model deployment works the same way.

cards.title: A smart doorbell cannot wait on a giant cloud model
cards.body: Imagine a doorbell camera that should spot a package instantly, even when the internet is weak. A huge model might be more accurate in theory, but a compact model that runs locally with low latency can be more useful because it answers fast and works offline.

cards.title: Highest accuracy is not always the winner
cards.body: A common myth is that the best model is always the one with the best benchmark score. But if that model is too slow, too expensive, or too large for the target device, it may lose in practice to a slightly less accurate model that actually fits the deployment budget.

cards.title: Cloud-scale vs. edge-ready
cards.body: The right model depends on where it has to run.

left.text:
More memory and compute available.
Can chase top accuracy.
Often slower and more expensive to serve at scale.

right.text:
Fits phones and small devices.
Lower latency and lower power use.
May trade away some accuracy to be practical.

exercises.prompt: Why do smaller models matter so much on phones and edge devices?

options.text: Because those devices have tighter memory, power, and latency limits.
Because edge devices are forbidden from using neural networks.
Because smaller models automatically need no training data.
Because large models cannot make predictions at all.

exercises.explain: Right. The issue is not that large models are impossible — it is that device budgets make them hard to deploy well.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If a model is slightly more accurate but far too slow for a live phone app, it is still automatically the better deployment choice.
exercises.explain: False. Deployment success depends on latency and device fit too, not only on raw accuracy.

exercises.sentence: Real-time apps often need low ___ so predictions appear quickly.
exercises.explain: Latency is correct. In many products, a late answer feels like a wrong answer.

exercises.prompt: Which deployment story best explains why smaller models matter?

options.text: A translation app needs answers in under a second on a phone, so the team prefers a lighter model that runs locally.
A model trains on a server, so deployment constraints never matter anywhere else.
A team chooses a bigger model only because its icon looks more impressive in slides.

exercises.explain: Exactly. The point is matching the model to the device and user experience it must support.

exercises.prompt: A wildlife camera in a forest has a tiny battery, weak internet, and must detect animals quickly on the device itself. Which model choice best fits that situation?

options.text: A compact model optimized for low memory use and low latency on the device.
The biggest available model, even if it needs constant cloud access and responds slowly.
No model at all, because edge devices cannot run neural networks.
A model chosen only by parameter count, without considering latency or power.

exercises.explain: Exactly. In edge deployment, the best model is the one that fits the hardware, latency, and power budget while still doing the job well enough.
